1 mutual group

You and Adi are both in AI & Machine Learning Community (moderated)

About

We're not AI for your email, we're email for your AI :)

Featured

  • Post

    My two best friends and I just raised $6 million to build AgentMail (YC S25).

    It's kinda crazy to see names like General Catalyst and YC who've put their trust and money in us to turn a dorm-room idea into a massive company.

    But it still just feels like the beginning. Through the past year, I've learned a lot and wanted to share some things that stood out.

    1) My hardest decision was made on day 1 - Michael Kim, Haakam Aujla, and I have shared everything: the same story, struggle, successes. We live together, work together, laugh together, fight together. No matter the highest highs or lowest lows, we always have each other's backs. I wouldn't do this with anyone else.

    2) Find happiness in being unhappy. I fell into the trap of thinking the next milestone would bring me peace. Once we get into YC, I'll take a break. Once we raise our seed, I'll take a break. No breaks were taken, and I'm still as unsatisfied as ever. But I've learned something. It's never going to be enough and time will just pass you by. Maybe that's the beauty of it? Maybe it's about enjoying the hardship, because the journey means more than the destination. Why not have fun along the way? I'm loving every moment of it.

    3) You can just do things. When we started AgentMail, I promised myself to think less. Yes, you read that right. Think less. Because if you think, you think about what people would say. You think about everything that could go wrong. You think about what happens if you lose. When I look at all we've
    accomplished by thinking less and doing more... I now think: what happens if we win?

    Thank you for being a part of our journey. See you at the next one :)

    786 · 216 comments

  • Post

    The best things in life are free. AgentMail is now free for everyone.

    306 · 98 comments

  • Post

    Thrilled to share that AgentMail will be a part of the Y Combinator S25 batch!

    A few months ago, we had an idea. What if the next big shift isn’t how the internet is used - it’s who uses it?

    The dominant user may soon be an AI Agent. In that case, the infrastructure that underpins everything on the web needs to be reimagined.

    We spotted the gap most clearly in email. Your inbox lets you connect, prove identity, and take action every single day, and yet, most agents today still borrow human accounts.

    So Haakam Aujla Michael Kim and I started tinkering.

    Our brainstorming became discovery. Our prototypes began going into prod by dozens of devs who faced the same problem - because Gmail was built for humans.

    That’s why AgentMail is the first email provider built for AI Agents. We aim to give every AI agent its own inbox, so that it can send, receive, and reason over email just like (or even better than) a human.

    We’re grateful for YC being an early believer. And even more grateful to the many developers, early adopters, and well-wishers reading this who’ve been there since this was nothing but an idea. Much more soon.

    601 · 193 comments

  • Post

    AgentMail (YC S25) is now hiring.

    The catch? We won't read a single resume - an AI agent will make all the decisions on our behalf.

    Think it will hire you? Send an email to hiring[at]agentmail[.]to

    Let me know how it goes in the comments!

    1,034 · 258 comments

  • Post

    AI broke our hiring agent. So we built one that might break hiring. If LLMs can be used to screen candidates out, they should also be used to get them in. That’s why we just launched recruiting[at]agentmail[.]to - 1,800+ live roles - Smart role matching - Resume reviews (and roasts) - Interview prep An email agent that works to get you hired 24/7 - for free.

    424 · 104 comments

  • Show all featured items

Activity

11,239 followers

  • Adi Singh

    • 2nd

    Co-Founder, AgentMail (YC S25)

    Visit my website

    3h •

    Something has changed in the way we think. Three years ago, Copilot started finishing our sentences. Cursor started understanding entire codebases. Then Claude and Codex said "hold my drink" and came after the IDE itself. They don't just complete. They plan, test, iterate, and execute. There's a dopamine hit to prompting your agent fix a bug in two hours when you could've done it in twenty minutes. I believe AI is now ingrained with the way we think. Human and AI cognition are becoming intertwined, and is starting with the fundamentals of building software. Almost poetically. Initially, this led me to think 24-29 year olds might be the most well primed. Young enough to have less to unlearn, old enough to have already learned the principles of programming. 1M views later on this tweet, I got quite a few reactions. Some impassioned, mostly from people outside the age range (LOL). And after thinking about it, the golden age isn't limited to an age at all. Pre-AI principles + post-AI execution applies to anyone. A pre-teen building a video game before ever taking a CS class. A retired teacher building the classroom app she's been sketching on napkins for years. Both real AgentMail users I've heard from over the past few weeks. Anyone who's passionate about anything can now do.. anything.

    52 reactions52

    5 comments5 comments

    •

    1 repost1 repost

    Comment
    Send

  • Adi Singh reposted this

    Binoy Perera

    • 2nd

    Founding Ops @ AgentMail YC S25 | Building the First Email Provider for AI Agents

    1w •

    Everybody wants to predict the death of SaaS.

    "AI is going to kill SaaS." "Nobody will pay for software subscriptions anymore." "The SaaS era is over."

    Yeah I'm not so sure.

    SaaS isn't dying. It's evolving. And the next form is already here and it might not have a login screen.

    Meet Headless SaaS, software that ditches the UI entirely. No dashboards. No onboarding flows. No tooltips. Just clean APIs that do the job. Think of it as the kitchen without the restaurant. The food is the same. The waiter is gone.

    Why? Because the fastest-growing "user" of your software isn't a person anymore. It's an AI agent.

    This is where B2A (Business-to-Agent) comes in.

    Your next customer might not care about fancy demos, brand colours etc.

    It's an agent acting on behalf of a human who never wants to touch your product directly.

    SaaS companies that get this will rebuild their products API-out. The ones that don't will keep polishing front doors that nobody walks through.

    Make something     #agents want!

    71 reactions71

    21 comments21 comments

    •

    3 reposts3 reposts

    Comment
    Send

  • Adi Singh

    • 2nd

    Co-Founder, AgentMail (YC S25)

    Visit my website

    6d •

    One question determines whether AgentMail (YC S25) lives or dies..

    We're a rare company in the sense that we've never changed, pivoted, or altered our idea since we started.

    We send millions of emails now but every part of our infra still follows the day 1 principle. Every agent needs its own email inbox.

      • Adi Singh

        • 2nd

        Co-Founder, AgentMail (YC S25)

        Visit my website

        1w •

        There were moments when it looked like AgentMail (YC S25) wasn’t going to work out.

        For most of the months we've been alive, we faced (and still face) the same question: “Why would an agent even need its own email inbox?”

        There weren’t many startups building agents, and the ones that were... would chose not to use us. Growth was stagnant, devs would quickly churn, and our momentum was fleeting at best.

        But we kept pushing.

        Until we found the first company that had such a big problem with Gmail.. they started running on AgentMail.
        Then we found the next ten.
        Then we kept going.

        What's hard to understand is that early on, the founders need to be the product.

        Your job is to push the idea forward, over and over, until you find the first person whose problem is solved, and then tell their story to the next ten, then the next million. These days, it seems like the goal is to build a movement before the vision that drives a company comes.

        If you have a vision for what the future looks like, now is the time to bet on it. To take that bet on yourself.

        99.99% of AI agents still don’t exist. Just keep going.
        Make something agents (should) want.

        355 reactions355

        59 comments59 comments

        •

        4 reposts4 reposts

        Comment
        Send

      • Adi Singh reposted this

        Josh Sirota

        • 2nd

        Eragon

        1w •

        Excited to announce Eragon $12M seed round.

        Led by Arielle Zuckerberg , Cyan Banister , and Lee J. at Long Journey .

        With support from Soma Capital , Axiom Partners , Roo Capital , and an incredible group of angels and partners including Elias Torres , Mike Knoop (Zapier Co-Founder), Deel and others world-class investors.

        Over the last several months we've been working with some of the fastest-growing companies in Silicon Valley and Fortune 500s as they move beyond generic AI and toward proprietary intelligence built for how their businesses actually run.

        We help companies own the intelligence that runs their business. The weights. The models. The judgment layer trained on their specific data. Intelligence that compounds privately, continuously, and never leaves their walls.

        If you're a fast growing startup looking to reduce costs with better performance head to eragon[dot]ai to learn more.

        If you want to help us build the future of enterprise intelligence, we're growing fast and hiring across the board.

        Thank you Tim and Techcrunch for covering our announcement.

        898 reactions898

        • 2nd

        Co-Founder, AgentMail (YC S25)

        Visit my website

        2w •

        My two best friends and I just raised $6 million to build AgentMail (YC S25).

        It's kinda crazy to see names like General Catalyst and YC who've put their trust and money in us to turn a dorm-room idea into a massive company.

        But it still just feels like the beginning. Through the past year, I've learned a lot and wanted to share some things that stood out.

        1) My hardest decision was made on day 1 - Michael Kim, Haakam Aujla, and I have shared everything: the same story, struggle, successes. We live together, work together, laugh together, fight together. No matter the highest highs or lowest lows, we always have each other's backs. I wouldn't do this with anyone else.

        2) Find happiness in being unhappy. I fell into the trap of thinking the next milestone would bring me peace. Once we get into YC, I'll take a break. Once we raise our seed, I'll take a break. No breaks were taken, and I'm still as unsatisfied as ever. But I've learned something. It's never going to be enough and time will just pass you by. Maybe that's the beauty of it? Maybe it's about enjoying the hardship, because the journey means more than the destination. Why not have fun along the way? I'm loving every moment of it.

        3) You can just do things. When we started AgentMail, I promised myself to think less. Yes, you read that right. Think less. Because if you think, you think about what people would say. You think about everything that could go wrong. You think about what happens if you lose. When I look at all we've
        accomplished by thinking less and doing more... I now think: what happens if we win?

        Thank you for being a part of our journey. See you at the next one :)

        786 reactions786

        216 comments216 comments

      • Adi Singh reposted this

        Francesco D'Alessio

        • 2nd

        Founder @ Tool Finder

        3w •

        We talk a lot about AI agents.

        But nobody talks about how they communicate.

        I just interviewed one of the co-founders of         AgentMail (YC S25).

        He impressed me with their concept.

        A Y Combinator backed startup that's building the first email provider designed entirely for AI agents.

        Think Gmail, but for your autonomous workflows.

        Some of the biggest AI companies are already building on top of it.

        We dig into why email is becoming the ideal interface for agents, how AgentMail works under the hood, and what it means for how humans and AI communicate going forward.

        Adi Singh - great to chat!
        Michael Kim, Haakam Aujla 💪🏼

          • Adi Singh

            • 2nd

            Co-Founder, AgentMail (YC S25)

            Visit my website

            3w •

            POV: It’s 1 AM at the AgentMail office. This week may have been our biggest one yet. We recorded all-time highs in daily and weekly sign ups, and partnered with Google and Coinbase in integrating with the ADK and the x402 protocol. Behind the scenes? An exceptionally small team of 20-something year olds with just one common goal: make something agents want. We work long hours, eat every meal together, and laugh a lot through it all. Because while it’s never easy, sometimes you don’t know when the good days are until you’ve left them :)

                2 reposts2 reposts

                Send

              • Adi Singh

                • 2nd

                Co-Founder, AgentMail (YC S25)

                Visit my website

                4w •

                A few weeks ago I met Ashton Kutcher at a lobster-themed AI agent conference. Yes, that is a real sentence. One of the things I'm most happy about learning since starting AgentMail is to just go up to people. Here's why: during my first weeks in SF, I saw Aravind Srinivas walk right past me while I was sitting on a park bench. I knew it was him. But then my same old internal monologue starts... is that really him? What would I even say if I went up? What if it's not him and I just look stupid? You find a million questions to doubt yourself with and by the time you're done, the moment's gone. I watched him walk away and drive off in an expensive black car with a funny license plate. Regretted it instantly. Then I did the exact same thing with Naval Ravikant at a Blue Bottle just a week later. It's hard to repeat mistakes more than once in these things. So now, if I see someone, I go up. And it's been awesome... in recent months, I've met founders of companies like Cognition, Ramp, Lovable. All from just taking a chance to start a conversation. So my advice: shoot your shot! You might confuse a random guy for Seth Rogen in a Sweetgreen but it's better than having any regrets :)

                151 reactions151

                18 comments18 comments

                Comment
                Send

              • Adi Singh reposted this

                Haakam Aujla

                • 2nd

                Co-Founder at AgentMail (YC S25)

                1mo •

                If you told my 14 year old self what happened in the past week, he would not believe you. If you told my 21 year old self, he also would not believe you.

                For the last year I have been busy building                 AgentMail (YC S25). In the last week alone I…

                1. Spoke at University of Michigan with YC in front of a crowd of 300+ student builders. Less than a year ago I was sitting in the same crowd.

                2. Watched YC partners discuss AgentMail on the Lightcone Podcast. I woke up to flood of notifications from our supporters and new customers.

                I remember watching YC on YouTube in 2017, thinking it would be a dream to be build a startup once I was all grown up. I have grown up a lot in the past year. And there is still a lot of growing to do.

                Thank you to Y Combinator and everyone else who has believed in us from the start.

                1/2

                256 reactions256
